Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd tell myself I'd study at certain times, but then I'd just... not feel like it. I spent maybe 24 hours a week on coursework, but it was rarely planned. I'd often watch lecture videos late at night when I couldn't sleep, or cram materials right before something was due. The sadness I've been dealing with made it hard to stick to any real schedule.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on deadlines and how I was feeling emotionally on any given day. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything until panic set in about an assignment. Other weeks, when the anxiety was really high, I'd actually study more just to try to quiet my mind. It wasn't healthy or consistent, but somehow I managed to complete most things eventually.
Q3: Usually what prompted me to log in was either a deadline notification or just guilt. I'd get an email reminder and think, okay, I really need to check what's happening. Sometimes I logged in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even if I ended up just scrolling through without really engaging. It was more about relieving anxiety than genuine interest.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a natural part of my routine. I'd go days without logging in, then have these intense sessions where I'd try to catch up on everything. The only thing that was somewhat routine was checking discussion boards occasionally because interacting with peers sometimes helped me feel less isolated in my sadness.
Q5: The video lectures were most useful for me because I'm a visual learner and I could pause and rewatch parts I didn't understand. I also found the discussion posts helpful because reading other students' perspectives made concepts click for me. When I actually engaged with these, I felt like I was learning something real, even if my attendance in actual classes was terrible.
Q6: I often ignored the readings and supplementary PDFs. They felt overwhelming when I was already struggling to get through the basics. I'd download them with good intentions but they'd just sit in a folder on my laptop. My anxiety made long text documents feel like mountains I couldn't climb, so I stuck to videos and discussions instead.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see an immediate connection to assignments or exams. If something felt abstract or disconnected from what I'd be graded on, my motivation just disappeared. I also valued anything that didn't require too much mental energy to start because getting started was always the hardest part for me.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford emotionally. Required ones came with deadlines and consequences, which honestly was the only thing that got me moving most days. Optional stuff just made me feel guilty for not doing it, which added to my stress. So I mostly avoided them to protect what little mental energy I had.
Q9: Yes, absolutely. I downloaded a lot of videos and lecture slides to my laptop and studied them offline. Sometimes I'd print things out and highlight them because staring at screens made my anxiety worse. I also used YouTube videos and external websites to understand concepts better when the course materials weren't clicking for me.
Q10: There were many times I'd be lying in bed reviewing printed notes or rewatching downloaded lectures on my tablet without any internet connection. I'd spend hours going over material in my head, trying to understand it, but none of that would show up anywhere. My actual learning often happened in these quiet, offline moments when I felt less pressured.
Q11: Definitely. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, download it, and then rewatch it multiple times offline over the following days. Same with lecture slides - I'd access them once to download, then study from the files constantly. The LMS would only show that single access, but I was actually engaging with that material repeatedly.
Q12: There were definitely periods where everything felt impossible. When my sadness was at its worst, I couldn't bring myself to attend classes or even log into the LMS. The anxiety would build up about falling behind, which made it even harder to start. It was this terrible cycle where the more I avoided things, the worse I felt.
Q13: Absolutely. When I had multiple deadlines across my eight online courses, I'd become paralyzed trying to figure out what to prioritize. The fatigue from poor sleep and constant low mood made everything take longer. Sometimes I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen, unable to actually do anything productive because I was so mentally exhausted.
Q14: Many times. I'd have videos playing in the background while my mind wandered to darker places. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. Sometimes I'd spend an hour on the LMS and realize I hadn't actually absorbed anything because I was too caught up in my own thoughts and sadness.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially tasks that required sustained concentration or creativity. The bigger the assignment, the more I'd avoid it because starting felt overwhelming. I knew they were important, but my brain would convince me I'd do it better tomorrow when I felt better. Tomorrow rarely came with the energy I hoped for.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd have intense bursts of activity right before things were due. The LMS would probably show me logging in frantically in the final hours before submission. Without deadlines, I'd drift indefinitely. They were stressful but necessary for someone like me who struggles with self-motivation.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback would give me a brief mood boost and motivate me to engage more. But critical feedback, even when constructive, would send me spiraling and make me avoid the course for days. My high anxiety made every piece of feedback feel like a judgment of my worth as a person.
Q18: The course structure with its clear deadlines helped because it forced me to engage at certain points. But the flexibility also meant I could skip classes without immediate consequences, which I did way too often. Having eight online courses simultaneously made it hard to feel connected to any single one. I became a ghost in most of them.
Q19: The video-heavy format made engagement easier because I could learn visually at my own pace. But the sheer amount of content across all my courses made it harder to keep up. Also, when discussion participation was required, it actually helped me engage more than I would have otherwise. Required peer interaction pushed me out of my isolation bubble.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who logs in frequently out of anxiety, watches videos, participates in discussions, but then struggles to actually produce work. The engagement might be genuine but scattered, or it might be someone going through the motions while dealing with personal issues. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually reflects my experience somewhat. My attendance was terrible and I didn't log in often, but I studied a lot offline and still managed to pass. I completed most assignments because I'd have these intense productive periods. Someone looking at my login frequency wouldn't guess I spent hours with downloaded materials and printed notes.
Q22: I think course logs can show patterns like when someone's active and what they click on. But they completely miss the quality of engagement and what's happening emotionally. They can't see that I'm crying while watching a lecture or that I've rewatched a downloaded video ten times. They show behavior, not learning or struggle.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks to get them done. It means thinking about course concepts outside of study time, connecting ideas together, and feeling some sense of investment in your own learning. For me, true engagement has been rare this semester because my emotional state keeps getting in the way.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my mental health struggles, the CBT sessions I've been attending, or how much effort it takes just to open my laptop some days. It wouldn't show the offline studying, the peer study sessions over text, or the times I helped classmates understand material. It definitely wouldn't show that passing this course while feeling this sad is actually a huge achievement for me.
